questions.questionText: Heberden's nodes (DIP joint swelling) and Bouchard's nodes (PIP joint swelling) are characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Osteoarthritis (degenerative joint disease — osteophytes at DIP=Heberden's, PIP=Bouchard's; RA spares DIP)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Heberden (DIP) + Bouchard (PIP) nodes = osteoarthritis. Why correct: OA (degenerative): non-inflammatory, joint cartilage wear, osteophyte formation. Heberden's nodes = DIP joint bony enlargements. Bouchard's nodes = PIP enlargements. Hand OA pattern: DIP > PIP > 1st CMC (thumb base). Why A wrong: RA = MCP + PIP involvement, SPARES DIP. Soft tissue swelling (synovitis), not bony nodes. Why C wrong: Gout = 1st MTP (podagra), tophi (urate deposits). Why D wrong: Psoriatic arthritis can affect DIP but with erosions (pencil-in-cup), not bony nodes. Clinical relevance: X-ray OA: joint space narrowing (asymmetric), subchondral sclerosis, osteophytes, subchondral cysts. Treatment: exercise, weight loss, paracetamol, NSAIDs, joint replacement. Exam trap: OA = DIP (Heberden's); RA = MCP/PIP (spares DIP). This DIP/MCP distinction is fundamental. Memory aid: 'Heberden = Highest (DIP); Bouchard = Below (PIP) = OA.' Reference: Maheshwari – Joint Disorders.
questions.conceptTag: OA Heberden DIP Bouchard PIP degenerative
